candidates.reasoning: The combination of white-washed stucco walls, Mediterranean scrubland on the hills, and the specific species of deciduous trees is highly characteristic of the Greek mainland or islands. The terrain is rocky and arid with sparse, low-lying vegetation typical of the Aegean region.
The landscape, soil color, and vegetation are nearly identical to Greece, sharing the same Eastern Mediterranean climate and settlement style of white-washed buildings against a dry, hilly backdrop.

The arid, hilly terrain and white-washed architecture are common in southern Spain (Andalusia). However, the specific tree types and road shoulder style are slightly more indicative of the Eastern Mediterranean.
Southern Italy and Sicily share this Mediterranean climate, rocky soil, and hilly terrain, though the white-washed aesthetic is more dominant in Greece and Spain.

As a cross-continent analog, certain regions of Mexico feature similar white-washed architecture and arid, hilly scrubland with deciduous trees, though the specific flora differs from the European Mediterranean.

The vegetation density and species composition are consistent with the Aegean or Ionian regions.
The presence of Platanus orientalis and the xeric, scrubby hillside vegetation are nearly identical to those found in Greece, as both share the same Eastern Mediterranean botanical province.

Southern Italy and Sicily feature similar Mediterranean vegetation and Platanus species, though the specific scrub density here is more characteristic of the Eastern Mediterranean.
The Mediterranean biome of California features similar xeric shrublands and the introduction of Platanus species (sycamores), providing a cross-continent analog for this vegetation type.
